spans.content: POSITIVITY IN EQUIVARIANT QUANTUM SCHUBERT CALCULUS
ABSTRACT. A conjecture of D. Peterson, proved by W. Graham [Gr], states that the structure constants of the
equivariant cohomology of a homogeneous space
satisfy a certain positivity property. In this paper we show that this positivity property holds in the more general situation of equivariant quantum cohomology.
It is well known that the (integral) cohomology of the homogeneous space
a connected, semisimple, complex Lie group and

a parabolic subgroup) satisfies a positivity property: its structure constants are nonnegative integers equal to the number of intersection points of three Schubert varieties, in general position, whose codimensions add up to the dimension of
Recently, Graham ([Gr]) has proved a conjecture of Peterson ([P]), asserting that
, enjoys a more general positivity property.
be the (positive) simple roots associated to
. These are determined by the equivariant Schubert varieties
defined with respect to the Borel group
, are indexed by the minimal length representatives
(see §2 below for more details). Assume, for simplicity, that
is of adjoint type. Then the equivariant cohomology of a point is equal to the polynomial ring
's are the negative simple roots in
(see §3 below). Graham's Theorem asserts that the coefficients
The aim of this paper is to show that a similar positivity result holds in the more general context of
equivariant quantum cohomology. This object was introduced by Givental and Kim ([GK]) for general groups

and it was initially used to compute presentations of the small quantum cohomology of (partial) flag manifolds ([GK, Kim1, Kim2, AS, Kim3]). Related ideas have played a fundamental role in Mirror Symmetry ([G]).
The equivariant quantum cohomology is a deformation of both the equivariant and quantum cohomology. It is a graded
is indexed by the roots in
in equivariant cohomology determines a basis denoted
in its quantum version. If we write
for the equivariant quantum multiplication, the product
are called equivariant Littlewood-Richardson coefficients (EQLR) and are polynomials in
. There is an effective algorithm to compute these coefficients if

is a Grassmannian ([Mil]), which is a consequence of an equivariant quantum Pieri/Monk rule (i.e., a multiplication with a divisor class). It is not clear from this algorithm that the EQLR coefficients enjoy any positivity property. The main result of this paper is that they do, and, as in the equivariant cohomology, this positivity holds in the more general context of homogeneous spaces
Theorem. The equivariant quantum Littlewood-Richardson coefficient
is a polynomial in the variables
The idea of proof is to use a projection formula to reduce the computation of the EQLR coefficient to a situation where we can apply Graham's positivity theorem ([Gr], Theorem 3.2).
Acknowledgments: I am grateful to my advisor, W. Fulton, whose suggestions improved the presentation of this paper. I also thank the referee for valuable comments.

are the (3-pointed, genus 0) Gromov-Witten invariants, equal to the number of rational curves
. This number is set to be equal to 0 if

The fact that such a multiplication gives an associative operation was proved, using algebro-geometric methods, in [KM] (also see [FP] and references therein). Computations and properties of the (small) quantum cohomology algebra were done e.g. in [W, Be, BCF, Po] for Grassmanians, [C, Ch, FGP, FW, Wo] for (partial) flag manifolds and in [KTa] for the Lagrangian and orthogonal Grassmannian. Recently, a simplification of the methods used to prove results about the quantum cohomology of the Grassmannian was achieved by Buch ([Bu1]). Similar ideas were since used in [Bu2, Bu3, BKT] for more general situations.

be Kontsevich' moduli space of stable maps. This is a projective algebraic variety of dimension

(for details see e.g. [FP]). Then the quantum LR coefficient is given by
